params.text:
When is restraint lawful?

‘Restraint’ under the MCA means using, or threatening to use, force to make a person do something they resist, or restricting their freedom of movement whether or not they resist. It includes physical holds, but also things like locking a door, using bed rails, covert medication or constant close supervision.

Restraint of a person who lacks capacity is only lawful when two conditions are both met:

  • Necessity — the person using restraint reasonably believes it is necessary to prevent harm to the person who lacks capacity.
  • Proportionality — the restraint is a proportionate response to both the likelihood of that harm and the seriousness of it.

Restraint must always be the least restrictive option that works, used for the shortest time possible, and never as punishment, for staff convenience, or because a service is short-staffed. Anything that goes beyond this can be abuse, and may amount to an unlawful deprivation of liberty.

params.taskDescription: Restraint of someone who lacks capacity is only lawful if two conditions are met. Drag each word into the correct gap.
params.textField: Restraint is only lawful when the worker reasonably believes it is *necessary* to prevent *harm* to the person, and where it is a *proportionate* response to both the likelihood and the *seriousness* of that harm. It must always be the *shortest* time possible and the least restrictive option.
